narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 41.3% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 36, Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles of 5 and 4 respectively, upper-middle family income, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is 37.27 and Year 3 decile is 5.91, giving context to how the model weighed your profile. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were your undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, and Year 3 decile, in that order of impact. Interestingly, your undergraduate GPA stood out as a strong positive force, pulling the prediction toward success since it's higher than the 3.24 average for bar passers, showing the model recognized your solid college performance. Your LSAT score also worked in your favor, contributing notably toward a passing prediction despite being slightly below the typical successful student's level. However, your Year 3 decile had the strongest pull in the opposite direction, significantly increasing the model's concern about bar failure, as it lags behind the higher deciles common among those who pass. Your full-time enrollment plan aligns well with the 93.2% of bar passers in similar status, and your family income fits the upper-middle bracket seen in 45.1% of successful students, but these played smaller roles here. Overall, while your GPA and LSAT helped balance things, the Year 3 decile tipped the scale enough to raise doubts about first-time bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many with similar starting points improve dramatically. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, strengthening your later-year grades to climb those deciles, and gathering letters from professors who can highlight your upward trajectory. You might also explore post-baccalaureate programs or targeted bar prep courses to demonstrate readiness. Reapplying next cycle with these enhancements could shift the model's view in your favor, opening doors to the full-time program you envision. Keep pushing; your upper-middle background and academic foundation position you well for that turnaround.
